nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated Andean foothill streams and rivers with rocky substrate, strong current, and clear, cool water; typical white-water and clear-water highland drainages of the Orinoco and Magdalena river systems

decorPreferences: smooth river rocks and slate for grazing surfaces
caves and rock overhangs as shelters
strong directional powerhead or circulation pump to simulate current
minimal fine substrate (coarse sand or fine gravel acceptable)
live or artificial plants to supplement biofilm surfaces
avoid sharp-edged decor that can abrade the underside

diet.notes: Dedicated aufwuchs-grazer — feeds primarily on algae, biofilm, and soft vegetable matter rasped from hard surfaces. Offer algae wafers, spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini/courgette, cucumber, spinach, peas), and sinking pellets with high vegetable content. Will accept soft-cooked sweet potato and squash. Occasional protein supplement (bloodworm, repashy) beneficial but must not dominate the diet. Grazes continuously throughout the day; ensure adequate algae/biofilm coverage on rocks and glass or supplement heavily with prepared foods.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras tolerant of cooler water (ember tetras, cardinal tetras at the upper range)
hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon) sharing similar flow and temperature requirements
cooler-water corydoras species (C. sterbai is too warm; prefer C. panda, C. trilineatus)
mountain cloud minnows (White Cloud Mountain Minnow)
other non-competing plecos of different size
avoidWith: tropical fish requiring temperatures above 26°C (discus, altum angels, most South American cichlids)
large aggressive cichlids that may attack the pleco
other Chaetostoma spp. or similarly shaped plecos competing for the same territory and grazing space in smaller tanks
fish requiring soft, still water (incompatible oxygenation needs)
fast-moving nippy species that harass the pleco’s fleshy fins

summary: The Rubber Pleco is a compact, matte-skinned Andean hillstream catfish with a broad bulldog head and powerful sucker mouth. It demands cool, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated water and a diet rich in algae and biofilm, distinguishing it from common tropical plecos. An intermediate-level species suited to cool-water community setups.
careNotes:
The Rubber Pleco (Chaetostoma milesi) originates from fast-flowing Andean foothill streams in Colombia and Venezuela, making it one of the more demanding pleco species with respect to water conditions. Unlike the ubiquitous Common Pleco or Bristlenose Pleco, it requires cool temperatures (20–24°C), high dissolved oxygen, and strong water movement — conditions closer to a hillstream loach setup than a typical tropical community tank. Temperatures above 26°C for extended periods are associated with lethargy, disease susceptibility, and premature death; this is the most common husbandry error reported with this species. A strong powerhead or circulation pump positioned to create meaningful surface agitation and directional flow is essential. High O₂ levels must be maintained, particularly during warm weather; a chiller may be necessary in summer for indoor setups in warm climates.

The Rubber Pleco is distinguished from the Bristlenose Pleco (Ancistrus spp.) by the absence of nasal tentacles (bristles), its distinctively broad and flattened “bulldog” head profile, and its thick matte, rubber-like skin texture. It is also stockier and less elongated than most other Loricariidae encountered in the hobby.

As a dedicated aufwuchs-grazer, this species feeds continuously on algae and biofilm throughout the day — constantGrazer behaviour. Tanks should ideally have well-established algae growth on rocks, glass, and driftwood before introduction. Supplement heavily with sinking algae wafers, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables (courgette, cucumber) as permanent fixtures in the tank. Protein-heavy feeding should be minimised; a plant-dominated diet mirrors its natural foraging ecology.

Provide ample smooth rocks, slate pieces, and driftwood for grazing surfaces and sheltering spots. The species is peaceful and can be kept singly or in pairs in larger tanks, though conspecific males may compete for prime grazing territory. A tank of at least 120 L (90 cm footprint) with excellent filtration and weekly water changes of 25–30% is recommended. Chaetostoma milesi is copper-sensitive in common with many catfishes; copper-based medications must be avoided.

See also: [bristlenose-pleco] (Ancistrus cirrhosus) — the common beginner-friendly pleco with nasal tentacles; [hillstream-loach] (Sewellia lineolata) — similar stream-dwelling grazer with analogous requirements.
breedingNotes: Breeding Chaetostoma milesi in captivity is uncommon and considered difficult. Spawning has been reported in well-maintained aquaria that closely replicate natural conditions: cool, highly oxygenated, fast-flowing water with a seasonal drop in temperature (to ~20°C) simulating Andean dry-season conditions. Eggs are deposited in caves or under flat rocks, with the male guarding the clutch. Fry are relatively large at hatching and begin grazing on biofilm and algae immediately. Commercial breeding is rare; most trade specimens are wild-collected. Hobbyists attempting breeding should provide multiple cave-type spawning sites, a species tank or separate breeding setup, and a diet rich in algae and vegetables to condition the adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chaetostoma milesi
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided native range (Colombia/Venezuela, Orinoco/Magdalena drainages), temperature range (20–24°C), pH 6.5–7.5, hardness, adult size (to ~12–14 cm), diet (aufwuchs, algae, biofilm), hillstream-loach-like requirements, and breeding notes. Strongly emphasises oxygenation and current requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Chaetostoma milesi
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Colombia, Venezuela), adult size, and diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs). Cross-referenced scientific name validity and distribution data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: General Loricariidae care context; confirmed diet recommendations (algae wafers, vegetables, biofilm), tank decor preferences (driftwood, rocks), and supplementary feeding guidance applicable to Chaetostoma species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Catfish of the Month: Chaetostoma species
additional.notes: Species profile covering Chaetostoma genus husbandry; confirmed cool-water, high-oxygenation requirements, aufwuchs-grazing behaviour, bulldog head morphology, and distinction from Ancistrus (no nasal tentacles). Notes on conspecific territorial behaviour in smaller tanks.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Chaetostoma milesi (CW004)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database entry; provided additional distribution data, hobbyist care reports, and confirmation of temperature sensitivity and oxygenation demands. Supplemented breeding difficulty assessment and note on wild-collection prevalence of trade specimens.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; requires cool (20°C), highly oxygenated, fast-flowing water with seasonal temperature drop to trigger spawning. Male guards eggs in caves. Most trade fish are wild-collected.
